text: Remembering a Jazz Legend today. He is one of my favourite artists, with bass clarinet and flute solos like human voices. His alto sax work is great too! Apart from his legendary albums like "Out to Lunch", live recordings with John Coltrane - Village Vanguard, Village Gate, Europe, etc. - are timeless too. He also did some great recordings with Ornette Coleman (Free Jazz), Charles Mingus (e.g. Antibes, Cornell, Europe). When Dolphy moved on, it probably influenced Coltrane's move to a quintet with Pharoah Sanders on board years later. Anyway, what are your favourites? Let's enjoy him today and always :)
label: appreciation
notes: Contains a question, but main purpose is first-person appreciation.

text: An absolutely formative experience for me. It was exactly what I was looking for back then. Still have VHS copies I made of it. Sun Ra's appearance is my favorite to this day. Probably among the most uncommercial shows in American commercial TV history, and God bless it for that. The wikipedia listing is excellent: Sunday Night, later named Michelob Presents Night Music, is a late-night television show which aired for two seasons between 1988 and 1990 as a showcase for jazz and eclectic musical artists.[4] It was hosted by David Sanborn. Jools Holland served as Sanborn's co-host for the first season; Sanborn hosted solo for the second. The show also featured a house band of Omar Hakim (drums), Marcus Miller (bass), Philippe Saisse (keyboards), David Sanborn (saxophone), Hiram Bullock (guitar), and Jools Holland (piano). Hal Willner was the music coordinator. And yeah, that studio band was outstanding. Tons of complete videos on youtube for it for those who haven't seen it.
label: appreciation
notes: Includes factual context, but main purpose is personal reaction.

text: I finally "got" Wayne Shorter's Speak No Evil last night after bouncing off it for years. The title track used to feel too shadowy to me, but now those horns sound like a whole city at 1am. Hubbard and Shorter together are just unreal. No big point here, just had one of those jazz moments where an album unlocks.
label: appreciation

text: Been on a Bill Evans at the Village Vanguard kick all week and it still floors me how conversational that trio sounds. LaFaro isn't just walking, he is practically writing little counter-melodies under every phrase. It feels fragile and completely alive at the same time.
label: appreciation

text: I don't know what clicked, but Alice Coltrane's Journey in Satchidananda went from "interesting" to deeply moving for me. The tambura, the harp, the bass line, Pharoah floating over it all... it feels like the room gets bigger every time I play it.
label: appreciation

text: Cannonball Adderley on Somethin' Else is the sound of pure good taste to me. He can be bluesy, playful, elegant, and absolutely burning without ever sounding like he is showing off. That record always improves my mood.
label: appreciation

text: I put on Kenny Burrell's Midnight Blue after a brutal work day and it did exactly what I needed. Warm guitar tone, congas sitting just right, Stanley Turrentine sounding like a streetlight in the rain. I swear that album lowers my blood pressure.
label: appreciation

text: The way Ahmad Jamal leaves space is still magic. Sometimes I hear younger pianists fill every inch of the bar, then I put on At the Pershing and remember how heavy a pause can be. That trio had such ridiculous balance.
label: appreciation

text: I was walking home in the rain listening to Coltrane's Crescent and the whole thing just hit me sideways. It is not as famous as A Love Supreme, but the emotional weight on that record is enormous. "Wise One" especially feels like somebody praying quietly.
label: appreciation

text: Lee Morgan's trumpet tone makes me grin like an idiot. Even when the tune is serious, there is this flash of personality in every entrance. The Sidewinder is fun, sure, but Search for the New Land is the one that really stays with me.
label: appreciation

text: Sarah Vaughan's voice on the ballads is almost unfair. She can make one note feel like it has three rooms inside it. I came to her late because I was mostly an instrumental jazz person, and now I feel like I missed a whole planet.
label: appreciation

text: Grant Green has become my comfort guitarist. Nothing too fussy, no wasted motion, just those clean lines that sit perfectly in the pocket. When I don't know what to play, I put on Idle Moments or Matador and everything settles down.
label: appreciation

text: Eric Dolphy's bass clarinet still sounds like it came from the future. I love that he can be angular and funny and heartbreaking in the same solo. Out to Lunch scared me the first time, but now it feels weirdly welcoming.
label: appreciation

text: Not a hot take, but Kind of Blue earns its reputation every single time. I tried to be contrarian about it when I was younger, and now I just admit that "Flamenco Sketches" is one of the most beautiful things humans have recorded.
label: appreciation
notes: Includes factual context, but main purpose is personal reaction.

text: I keep returning to Paul Desmond when I want melody without drama. His tone is so dry and calm, but never boring. There is something about those lines over Jim Hall's guitar that feels like a very smart friend telling a quiet joke.
label: appreciation

text: Saw a local piano trio in a tiny bar last night and it reminded me why this music works best up close. No famous names, no fancy stage, just three people listening hard to each other. The drummer caught every little rhythmic nudge. Beautiful stuff.
label: appreciation

text: Mingus's "Goodbye Pork Pie Hat" hits me differently every year. When I first heard it, I just thought it was pretty. Now it feels like grief, dignity, anger, and tenderness all braided together. What a composer.
label: appreciation

text: I used to think big band was background music because I only knew the obvious swing-era stuff. Then I heard Ellington at Newport and some Thad Jones/Mel Lewis charts and realized how much color and tension can live in a large ensemble.
label: appreciation

text: McCoy Tyner's left hand might be my favorite force of nature in jazz. Those quartal voicings feel like doors opening. Even when the tempo is flying, there is this grounded, almost architectural thing happening.
label: appreciation

text: Art Blakey records make chores impossible because I always end up air-drumming instead of doing anything useful. That press roll into a horn shout feels like caffeine. The Jazz Messengers are basically instant momentum.
label: appreciation

text: Chet Baker's vocal records have that fragile, almost-too-close quality that I understand is not for everyone, but they really get to me. He sounds less like he is performing a song and more like he is barely admitting it.
label: appreciation

text: I cannot stop thinking about Mary Lou Williams's piano playing. There is so much history in it, but it never feels museum-like. She can sound stride, modern, churchy, and completely her own within a few bars.
label: appreciation

text: Sonny Rollins on Saxophone Colossus feels like someone thinking out loud at superhuman speed. "Blue 7" especially has that quality where every idea answers the one before it. I love how logical and playful he is at once.
label: appreciation

text: Dexter Gordon's tone makes every phrase feel like it is leaning back in a chair. I know that is a cliche, but there is no better way to describe it. Go is such a relaxed masterpiece.
label: appreciation

text: Nina Simone doing "I Loves You Porgy" completely stops time for me. She has this way of making the piano part and the vocal part feel like two sides of the same ache. I never put it on casually.
label: appreciation

text: Horace Silver always makes me feel like the sun came out. The tunes are catchy, but not simple. That mix of blues, church, Latin rhythm, and hard bop snap is just ridiculously satisfying.
label: appreciation

text: Had one of those late-night moments with In a Silent Way where I forgot I was listening to "fusion" and just floated. The editing, the electric piano wash, Miles barely saying anything but saying exactly enough. What a mood.
label: appreciation

text: The first time I heard Clifford Brown, I honestly got a little annoyed because nobody should sound that clean and that warm at the same time. Every solo feels generous. There is no cynicism in his playing.
label: appreciation

text: There is something about Geri Allen's touch that gets under my skin. She can be percussive without getting hard, lyrical without getting soft. I wish I had discovered her much earlier.
label: appreciation

text: I know some folks find Keith Jarrett's vocalizing distracting, and fair enough, but when the trio is locked in I barely notice it. Standards Vol. 1 has such insane listening between the players. It feels like a living organism.
label: appreciation

text: Charlie Parker still sounds shocking to me. Not "important historical figure" shocking, but actually shocking. Those lines just keep turning corners faster than my brain can follow, and somehow they still sing.
label: appreciation

text: I put on "Idle Moments" for background music and immediately failed at having it in the background. That title track is so patient. Every solo has room to breathe, and the groove feels like it could go on forever.
label: appreciation

text: A Love Supreme has become less of an album and more of a place I visit. I don't play it constantly, but when I do, it resets something in me. Elvin's cymbals alone are worth sitting still for.
label: appreciation

text: Spent the morning with Ella and Louis and honestly it is hard to be in a bad mood afterward. The contrast between those voices is perfect. She is all silk and precision, he is gravel and sunshine, and somehow it becomes one thing.
label: appreciation

text: Pharoah Sanders's tone on Karma feels like a cry and a celebration at the same time. I get why it can be too much for some people, but for me it is the opposite of chaos. It feels honest.
label: appreciation

text: Oscar Peterson's swing is almost suspicious. He makes impossible runs feel like he just thought of them while smiling at somebody across the room. Night Train is one of my go-to records when I need elegance with muscle.
label: appreciation

text: The Carla Bley stuff I ignored for too long is now some of my favorite music. Funny, dry, strange, beautifully arranged, never trying to impress you in the obvious way. Escalator Over the Hill is a whole universe.
label: appreciation

text: Ben Webster's breathy sound on ballads gets me every time. It is not just tone, it is timing. He can make a phrase feel like it is arriving from across the room with its hat in its hands.
label: appreciation

text: Joe Henderson's Inner Urge has been living in my head. There is something about his phrasing that feels slippery but never vague. He can duck around the beat and still sound completely centered.
label: appreciation

text: I have a soft spot for Jimmy Smith records because they sound like a room full of people having a much better evening than you. The organ trio format can get cheesy fast, but when it works, it really works.
label: appreciation

text: Monk's solo piano feels like watching someone build furniture with a hammer and a sly sense of humor. The wrong notes are not wrong, the pauses are not empty, and the whole thing somehow dances.
label: appreciation

text: The Sun Ra Arkestra makes me happy in a way almost nothing else does. Even the messy parts feel intentional because the whole idea is bigger than polish. Joyful, strange, serious, silly, cosmic, all at once.
label: appreciation

text: I was not ready for Betty Carter. The way she bends time around a lyric is wild. Sometimes it sounds like she is daring the band to catch her, and then of course they do.
label: appreciation

text: Brazilian jazz has been saving my week. Getz/Gilberto is the obvious doorway, but Milton Banana Trio and Tamba Trio have this lightness that never feels thin. The groove just floats.
label: appreciation

text: Andrew Hill's Point of Departure is one of those records that makes me feel smarter and more confused at the same time. The writing is knotty, but the emotion is right there if you stop trying to solve it.
label: appreciation

text: I love when Ornette Coleman sounds like he is laughing through the horn. People describe the music as difficult, but a lot of it feels incredibly human to me. Like melodies chasing each other around a playground.
label: appreciation

text: "Naima" still stops me cold. I have heard it a hundred times, and that first calm statement of the melody still feels private, like you walked into a room you should be quiet in.
label: appreciation

text: Listening to Shirley Horn late at night is dangerous because suddenly everything else feels too loud. Her time is so slow and confident. She can hold a note or a silence until the whole room leans forward.
label: appreciation

text: I keep coming back to Tony Williams because even at his most explosive he sounds like he is composing on the drums. The ride cymbal, the accents, the way he pushes without flattening the band. Unreal.
label: appreciation

text: Just wanted to say jazz radio at 2am is still one of life's small miracles. You tune in half-asleep and suddenly some obscure tenor player from 1961 is making the kitchen feel sacred.
label: appreciation
notes: Includes factual context, but main purpose is personal reaction.

text: I've listened to this album well over 500 times (literally) and it doesn't get boring. Such refined, elegant compositions with fantastic record quality itself (as it should be for ECM). Totally recommend you give it a listen if you don't know this album Apple Music: https://music.apple.com/us/album/spark-of-life/1443259469 YouTube Music: https://music.youtube.com/playlist?list=OLAK5uy_m-8VsyTgmXQqwRNPfLEbgmrMcsgBi6UD8 CD: https://ecmrecords.com/product/spark-of-life-marcin-wasilewski-trio-joakim-milder/
label: recommendation
notes: Question-like wording, but main purpose is recommending music.

text: If you have never heard Bobby Hutcherson's Happenings, do yourself a favor and put it on with headphones. Herbie Hancock is on piano, Joe Chambers is on drums, and the whole record has this cool-blue floating quality without losing swing.
label: recommendation

text: Please give Mal Waldron's Left Alone a listen if you like piano that sounds spare, haunted, and completely unsentimental. It is not a flashy record, but it stays in the room after it ends.
label: recommendation

text: For anyone who likes quiet late-night piano trio albums, I strongly recommend Bobo Stenson's Serenity. It has that ECM spaciousness, but the playing never turns into wallpaper.
label: recommendation

text: Strong recommendation: Joe Henderson's Page One. "Blue Bossa" is the famous tune, but the whole album is concise, melodic, and a perfect doorway into Henderson's sound.
label: recommendation

text: If you are curious about jazz vocals beyond the obvious names, try Abbey Lincoln's Abbey Is Blue. It has bite, sadness, intelligence, and Max Roach's presence all over it.
label: recommendation

text: Put on Charles Tolliver's Paper Man if you want trumpet playing with a big, searching, post-bop edge. The compositions are strong and the band has that late-60s urgency.
label: recommendation

text: For a rainy Sunday, I cannot recommend Jim Hall's Concierto enough. The version of "Concierto de Aranjuez" is long, patient, and ridiculously beautiful without turning syrupy.
label: recommendation

text: Modern rec: Immanuel Wilkins - Omega. It feels connected to the tradition without sounding like homework, and the writing gives the quartet room to breathe and burn.
label: recommendation

text: If fusion has never clicked for you, try Herbie Hancock's Thrust before giving up. It grooves hard, the synth textures are funky instead of cheesy, and Mike Clark is ridiculous.
label: recommendation

text: Check out Dorothy Ashby's Afro-Harping if you want something elegant, funky, and a little unexpected. Jazz harp should not work this well in a groove setting, but it absolutely does.
label: recommendation

text: Anyone wanting trumpet with edge should hear Woody Shaw's Rosewood. The arrangements are dense in a good way, and Shaw's lines feel like they are cutting new angles through the changes.
label: recommendation

text: Sonny Clark - Cool Struttin' is an easy recommendation for hard bop beginners. Great tunes, perfect Blue Note feel, and Jackie McLean adds just enough bite to keep it from getting too polite.
label: recommendation

text: For people who like Mingus and want somewhere to go next, try Booker Ervin's The Freedom Book. It has that earthy, urgent, gospel-meets-fire feeling and the band is all in.
label: recommendation

text: Give Andrew Hill's Smokestack a shot, especially if you like records that feel slightly off-center. Two basses, weird gravity, and Hill's piano making everything tilt in the best way.
label: recommendation

text: Late-night headphones recommendation: Kenny Wheeler's Gnu High. Keith Jarrett, Dave Holland, and Jack DeJohnette are all on it, but Wheeler's writing is the real star.
label: recommendation

text: If you enjoy Bill Evans but want something a little less familiar, try Enrico Pieranunzi's Live in Paris. Lyrical, swinging, and full of those little harmonic turns that reward repeat listening.
label: recommendation

text: Do not sleep on Harold Land's The Fox. It is direct, fiery, and way less talked about than it should be. Dupree Bolton's trumpet alone makes it worth hearing.
label: recommendation

text: For a clean entry into freer jazz, I recommend Ornette Coleman's Change of the Century. The melodies are catchy, the band is loose but focused, and it is not nearly as scary as its reputation.
label: recommendation
notes: Personal enthusiasm included, but main purpose is recommendation.

text: Check out the Chico Hamilton Quintet with Eric Dolphy if you want chamber-jazz textures that still move. Dolphy's flute and reeds make the group sound unlike almost anything else from the period.
label: recommendation

text: If you want a standards record that never feels routine, listen to Keith Jarrett Trio's Still Live. The band stretches familiar tunes until they feel freshly discovered.
label: recommendation

text: For bass lovers, Charlie Haden's Nocturne is gorgeous. Boleros, warm tone, beautiful guests, and a relaxed mood that still has real emotional weight.
label: recommendation

text: Try Tigran Hamasyan's Mockroot if you like jazz, prog, Armenian folk rhythm, and piano that sounds like it might take flight. It is intense but very rewarding.
label: recommendation

text: John Zorn's Masada albums are worth hearing if you want short, sharp tunes with klezmer flavor and serious improvising. Start with Alef or Live in Jerusalem and go from there.
label: recommendation

text: Need a joyful record? Cannonball Adderley's Mercy, Mercy, Mercy! Live at "The Club" is pure energy. Even knowing it was recorded in a studio, the vibe is infectious.
label: recommendation
notes: Question-like wording, but main purpose is recommending music.

text: If you only know Nina Simone as a singer, listen closely to her piano on Little Girl Blue. The classical touch, the swing feel, the little harmonic choices - she was a monster musician.
label: recommendation

text: Recommendation for guitar fans: Grant Green's Matador. It has McCoy Tyner and Elvin Jones, so the rhythm section has that Coltrane-quartet lift under Green's clean single-note lines.
label: recommendation

text: For spiritual jazz without going too far into the deep end, try McCoy Tyner's Extensions. Alice Coltrane is on harp, Wayne Shorter is there, and the ensemble sound is huge.
label: recommendation

text: If you liked Kamasi Washington but want to explore an older source, listen to Pharoah Sanders's Thembi. It is expansive and spiritual, but also full of memorable grooves and textures.
label: recommendation

text: Go listen to Wynton Kelly's Kelly Blue if you need a reminder that swinging hard can be its own kind of profundity. The title track is a perfect small-group performance.
label: recommendation

text: For a punchy modern trio, I recommend The Bad Plus - These Are the Vistas. The covers got attention, but the originals and group chemistry are the real reason to hear it.
label: recommendation
notes: Personal enthusiasm included, but main purpose is recommendation.

text: If Duke Ellington feels too huge to approach, start with Ellington Indigos. It is moody, polished, and full of beautiful writing without requiring a graduate seminar first.
label: recommendation

text: Thelonious Monk Plays Duke Ellington is a great recommendation for anyone still warming up to Monk. Familiar tunes make it easier to hear how personal his rhythm and voicings are.
label: recommendation

text: For people who want jazz that grooves but still has serious improvising, Eddie Harris and Les McCann's Swiss Movement is a blast. "Compared to What" alone justifies the record.
label: recommendation

text: This sub should talk more about Rene Marie. Start with Vertigo if you want a vocalist who can be theatrical, swinging, funny, and emotionally direct without sounding like anyone else.
label: recommendation

text: If you are intimidated by Cecil Taylor, try Jazz Advance before jumping into the later storms. You can hear the roots clearly, but the angles are already there.
label: recommendation

text: Check out Milt Jackson and Wes Montgomery's Bags Meets Wes! if you want a warm, bluesy, beautifully recorded session. Vibes and guitar together can be such a good texture.
label: recommendation

text: For a gorgeous sax/piano duo album, I recommend Lee Konitz and Brad Mehldau's Alone Together. It is patient, conversational, and full of tiny surprises.
label: recommendation
notes: Personal enthusiasm included, but main purpose is recommendation.

text: I always recommend Herbie Hancock's Speak Like a Child to people who want something lyrical but not sleepy. The horn colors are unusual and the compositions feel quietly luminous.
label: recommendation
notes: Personal enthusiasm included, but main purpose is recommendation.

text: If you want early jazz that does not sound dusty, listen to Louis Armstrong's Hot Fives and Sevens with real attention. The sound is old, but the imagination is still electric.
label: recommendation

text: For late-Coltrane-curious listeners, try Transition before diving straight into the wildest stuff. You can hear the classic quartet stretching toward something larger in real time.
label: recommendation

text: Try Yussef Dayes - Black Classical Music if you want a modern record with jazz drumming, London groove, and a lot of atmosphere. It is long, but it keeps opening new rooms.
label: recommendation

text: If you need something calming but not sleepy, put on Paul Desmond and Jim Hall's Take Ten. Desmond's tone plus Hall's comping is basically civilized conversation as music.
label: recommendation

text: Record store find to recommend: Hampton Hawes - For Real! It has Harold Land and Scott LaFaro, and the whole thing swings with a West Coast bite that deserves more attention.
label: recommendation

text: The Art Ensemble of Chicago's Nice Guys is my recommendation for people who want avant-garde jazz with humor, space, percussion, and actual tunes peeking through the abstraction.
label: recommendation

text: For vocal harmony in jazz, try Lambert, Hendricks & Ross - The Hottest New Group in Jazz. The vocalese thing may sound gimmicky on paper, but the execution is unbelievably hip.
label: recommendation

text: If you like cinematic jazz, check out Piero Umiliani's soundtrack work, especially the jazzier cues. It is Italian film music, but the arrangements and grooves have real charm.
label: recommendation

text: Please hear Elvin Jones - Puttin' It Together if you mostly know him from Coltrane. Joe Farrell and Jimmy Garrison are there, and Elvin sounds massive in a smaller setting.
label: recommendation

text: For anyone exploring Brazilian jazz, I recommend Tamba Trio's Avanco. The vocals, piano, and rhythm section are tight, bright, and harmonically much richer than casual bossa background music.
label: recommendation
notes: Personal enthusiasm included, but main purpose is recommendation.

text: My easy recommendation for people coming from hip-hop is Madlib's Shades of Blue, then follow the samples back to the original Blue Note records. It is a fun doorway into the catalog.
label: recommendation
notes: Personal enthusiasm included, but main purpose is recommendation.

text: Cartoon in memory of Abdullah Ibrahim (Dollar Brand) by Zapiro: See here for original: https://www.dailymaverick.co.za/cartoon/2026-06-18-hamba-kahle-1/ RIP...
label: information
notes: Includes link/suggestion language, but main purpose is information.

text: Billie Holiday — “Crazy He Calls Me”Decca 24796 (U.S.) Matrix W7542T2A - 78 rpm Recorded 1949: A late‑1940s Decca session featuring Billie Holiday at her most intimate and expressive. Written by Carl Sigman and Bob Russell, “Crazy He Calls Me” became one of Holiday’s signature ballads — a slow‑burning, emotionally rich performance that shows her mastery of phrasing and nuance.This original Decca Personality Series pressing captures that warm, velvety studio sound typical of the label’s post‑war era. Holiday’s voice floats over a restrained orchestral backing, giving the song its timeless, late‑night glow.From The Neven 78 Radio Archive — preserving historic jazz, blues, and popular music on original shellac. https://youtube.com/@mamlishblues?si=7HFH_xEx0PVcJLJR
label: information

text: A powerhouse improvisation from Sidney Bechet, one of the most commanding voices in early jazz. “Saturday Night Blues” captures Bechet in full flight — rich tone, fierce vibrato, and that unmistakable melodic authority that made him a legend on both clarinet and soprano sax.This early Blue Note release comes from the label’s formative years, when Alfred Lion and Francis Wolff were documenting some of the most vital small‑group jazz in New York. The 767 Lexington Ave. address places this disc firmly in the pre‑modern Blue Note era, prized by collectors for its raw, intimate sound. https://youtube.com/@mamlishblues?si=HGWylRM5llstOdoa
label: information

text: Smalls Jazz Club in NYC has an extensive free online archive of thousands of live jazz shows that have performed there. It's worth checking out. https://www.smallslive.com/search/archive/
label: information
notes: Includes link/suggestion language, but main purpose is information.

text: John Coltrane's Blue Train was recorded for Blue Note on September 15, 1957, with Lee Morgan, Curtis Fuller, Kenny Drew, Paul Chambers, and Philly Joe Jones. It remains Coltrane's only session as leader for the label and one of the defining hard bop LPs of the late 1950s.
label: information

text: Miles Davis's Kind of Blue was recorded at Columbia's 30th Street Studio in New York over two 1959 sessions. The album's modal approach gave players like John Coltrane, Cannonball Adderley, Bill Evans, and Wynton Kelly room to build solos from scales rather than dense chord changes.
label: information

text: The Newport Jazz Festival began in 1954 in Newport, Rhode Island, founded by George Wein and Elaine Lorillard. Duke Ellington's 1956 appearance, especially the long Paul Gonsalves solo during "Diminuendo and Crescendo in Blue," helped restore Ellington's mainstream visibility.
label: information

text: Blue Note's classic visual identity was shaped by designer Reid Miles and photographer Francis Wolff during the 1950s and 1960s. The bold typography, cropped session photos, and limited color palettes became almost as recognizable as the label's recorded sound.
label: information

text: Ella Fitzgerald's Song Book series for Verve started in the mid-1950s and covered composers including Cole Porter, Rodgers and Hart, Duke Ellington, Irving Berlin, and the Gershwins. The albums helped define the Great American Songbook for LP-era listeners.
label: information

text: Charles Mingus recorded Mingus Ah Um for Columbia in 1959, the same year as Kind of Blue and Time Out. Tracks like "Goodbye Pork Pie Hat," "Fables of Faubus," and "Better Git It in Your Soul" show his mix of blues, gospel, politics, and arranged ensemble writing.
label: information

text: Thelonious Monk's Brilliant Corners was recorded for Riverside in 1956 and required multiple takes because of the title track's difficult structure. The final master was edited together from several takes, with Sonny Rollins, Ernie Henry, Oscar Pettiford, Max Roach, and others involved.
label: information

text: Charlie Parker's Savoy and Dial recordings from the 1940s document bebop in its formative years. Sides such as "Ko-Ko," "Now's the Time," and "Ornithology" show Parker's fast harmonic thinking, blues grounding, and influence on nearly every alto player after him.
label: information

text: Art Blakey's Jazz Messengers became one of jazz's major training grounds after the mid-1950s. Alumni included Lee Morgan, Wayne Shorter, Freddie Hubbard, Wynton Marsalis, Cedar Walton, and many others, making the band both a working group and a school.
label: information

text: Bill Evans Trio's Sunday at the Village Vanguard was recorded live on June 25, 1961, with Scott LaFaro and Paul Motian. LaFaro died in a car accident less than two weeks later, giving the recordings an added historical weight.
label: information

text: Ornette Coleman's The Shape of Jazz to Come was recorded in 1959 for Atlantic with Don Cherry, Charlie Haden, and Billy Higgins. Its piano-less quartet format and looser harmonic method made it a landmark in the early development of free jazz.
label: information

text: Alice Coltrane's Ptah, the El Daoud was recorded in 1970 and features Pharoah Sanders and Joe Henderson on reeds, plus Ron Carter and Ben Riley. The album mixes modal jazz, harp, piano, and spiritual atmosphere in a compact small-group setting.
label: information

text: Herbie Hancock's Maiden Voyage was recorded for Blue Note in 1965 with Freddie Hubbard, George Coleman, Ron Carter, and Tony Williams. The nautical theme ties together compositions like "Maiden Voyage," "The Eye of the Hurricane," and "Dolphin Dance."
label: information

text: Duke Ellington premiered Black, Brown and Beige at Carnegie Hall in 1943 as an extended work about African American history. Though the original reception was mixed, later versions and excerpts became important examples of Ellington's large-scale compositional ambition.
label: information

text: Mary Lou Williams's Zodiac Suite began as a set of pieces associated with astrological signs and was performed in the mid-1940s, including a Carnegie Hall presentation in 1946. The music connects swing-era language with modern harmony and chamber-like structure.
label: information

text: Bossa nova entered the U.S. jazz mainstream in the early 1960s through recordings by Stan Getz, Charlie Byrd, Joao Gilberto, and Antonio Carlos Jobim. Getz/Gilberto, released in 1964, became one of the best-known crossover albums in jazz history.
label: information

text: ECM Records was founded in Munich in 1969 by producer Manfred Eicher. The label became known for spacious engineering, distinctive cover design, and artists such as Keith Jarrett, Jan Garbarek, Chick Corea, Pat Metheny, and the Art Ensemble of Chicago.
label: information

text: Max Roach's We Insist! Freedom Now Suite was released by Candid in 1960 and includes Abbey Lincoln, Coleman Hawkins, Olatunji, and others. The album directly addresses civil rights and African independence movements through composed sections and intense improvisation.
label: information

text: Weather Report's Heavy Weather, released in 1977, includes "Birdland" and features Joe Zawinul, Wayne Shorter, Jaco Pastorius, Alex Acuna, and Manolo Badrena. It became one of the most commercially successful jazz fusion albums of the decade.
label: information

text: Sarah Vaughan's mid-1950s EmArcy recordings, including Sarah Vaughan with Clifford Brown, paired her voice with major modern jazz players. The 1954 Brown session is often cited for its combination of vocal control, bebop phrasing, and small-group intensity.
label: information

text: The Clifford Brown and Max Roach Quintet operated from 1954 until Brown's death in 1956. With Harold Land or Sonny Rollins on tenor, Richie Powell on piano, and George Morrow on bass, the group became a model for hard bop small-group playing.
label: information

text: Louis Armstrong's Hot Five and Hot Seven recordings from 1925 to 1928 are central documents of early jazz. They shifted attention toward the improvised soloist, with Armstrong's trumpet, timing, and vocal personality setting a new standard.
label: information

text: Coleman Hawkins recorded "Body and Soul" in 1939, largely improvising around the song's harmony rather than stating the melody directly. The record became a major example of how a popular song could become a vehicle for modern jazz improvisation.
label: information

text: The Village Vanguard opened in Greenwich Village in 1935 and became one of New York's most important jazz clubs. Classic live recordings there include sessions by Sonny Rollins, John Coltrane, Bill Evans, Keith Jarrett, and many others.
label: information

text: Sun Ra moved from Birmingham to Chicago and later built the Arkestra into one of jazz's most unusual long-running ensembles. His work combined swing, free improvisation, electronic keyboards, poetry, costumes, and a self-created cosmic mythology.
label: information

text: Art Blakey and the Jazz Messengers' Moanin' was recorded for Blue Note in 1958 with Lee Morgan, Benny Golson, Bobby Timmons, and Jymie Merritt. Timmons's title track became a hard bop anthem with a strong gospel-blues feel.
label: information

text: The Dave Brubeck Quartet's Time Out was released by Columbia in 1959 and focused on unusual meters. "Take Five," composed by Paul Desmond in 5/4, became one of the rare jazz instrumentals to reach broad popular recognition.
label: information

text: Jazz at Massey Hall was recorded in Toronto in 1953 with Charlie Parker, Dizzy Gillespie, Bud Powell, Charles Mingus, and Max Roach. The concert is often described as one of the last major documents of the bebop founders together.
label: information

text: Jaco Pastorius's self-titled 1976 debut album features both electric bass showcases and arranged ensemble pieces. Tracks like "Donna Lee" and "Continuum" helped establish Pastorius as a defining voice on fretless electric bass.
label: information

text: Grant Green's Idle Moments was recorded for Blue Note in 1963 with Joe Henderson, Bobby Hutcherson, Duke Pearson, Bob Cranshaw, and Al Harewood. The extended title track is known for its relaxed tempo and spacious soloing.
label: information

text: Dexter Gordon's Go was recorded for Blue Note in 1962 with Sonny Clark, Butch Warren, and Billy Higgins. The album captures Gordon after his return to prominence, with a relaxed tenor sound and direct versions of standards and blues.
label: information

text: Wayne Shorter's Speak No Evil was recorded in 1964 with Freddie Hubbard, Herbie Hancock, Ron Carter, and Elvin Jones. Its compositions use memorable melodies and ambiguous harmonies, making it a key document of 1960s post-bop writing.
label: information

text: The Association for the Advancement of Creative Musicians, or AACM, was founded in Chicago in 1965. Members including Muhal Richard Abrams, Roscoe Mitchell, Anthony Braxton, Henry Threadgill, and the Art Ensemble of Chicago expanded the language of experimental jazz.
label: information

text: Verve Records was founded by Norman Granz in 1956, partly to provide a home for Ella Fitzgerald's recordings. The label also documented major work by artists including Oscar Peterson, Billie Holiday, Stan Getz, and many others.
label: information

text: Lee Morgan's The Sidewinder was recorded for Blue Note in 1963 and became an unexpected commercial hit. Its title track blended hard bop improvising with a catchy boogaloo-style groove that helped influence later soul-jazz records.
label: information

text: John Coltrane's A Love Supreme was recorded in December 1964 with McCoy Tyner, Jimmy Garrison, and Elvin Jones. The four-part suite was released by Impulse! in 1965 and is widely treated as a central spiritual statement in jazz.
label: information

text: Billie Holiday recorded "Strange Fruit" for Commodore in 1939 after Columbia declined to release it. Written by Abel Meeropol, the song's anti-lynching text and Holiday's restrained delivery made it one of the most significant protest recordings in American music.
label: information

text: Sonny Rollins's The Bridge, released in 1962, marked his return after a period of withdrawal from recording and public performance. The title refers partly to his practice sessions on the Williamsburg Bridge in New York.
label: information

text: McCoy Tyner's The Real McCoy was recorded for Blue Note in 1967 after his years with John Coltrane's classic quartet. With Joe Henderson, Ron Carter, and Elvin Jones, the album presents Tyner's modal language in a strong leader setting.
label: information

text: Candid Records documented several politically and artistically bold jazz projects around 1960 and 1961. The catalog includes work by Charles Mingus, Max Roach, Abbey Lincoln, Booker Little, Cecil Taylor, and others during a short but influential period.
label: information

text: Ken Burns's Jazz documentary aired on PBS in 2001 as a multi-part history of the music. The series brought archival footage and interviews to a broad audience, while also sparking debate about which later styles and artists received less attention.
label: information

text: Joe Henderson's Page One was recorded for Blue Note in 1963 with Kenny Dorham, McCoy Tyner, Butch Warren, and Pete La Roca. It introduced Henderson as a leader and includes Dorham's widely played composition "Blue Bossa."
label: information

text: Ahmad Jamal's At the Pershing: But Not for Me was recorded live in Chicago in 1958. The album's version of "Poinciana" became especially popular, highlighting Jamal's use of space, dynamics, and tight trio arrangements.
label: information

text: Oscar Peterson recorded Night Train for Verve in 1962 with Ray Brown and Ed Thigpen. The album uses short, blues-centered performances and became one of Peterson's most accessible trio records without sacrificing his technical command.
label: information

text: Cannonball Adderley's Mercy, Mercy, Mercy! Live at "The Club" was released in 1967 and features Joe Zawinul's hit title tune. Despite the club framing, the recording was made before an invited audience in a Capitol studio.
label: information

text: The Tony Williams Lifetime's Emergency! was released in 1969 and features Williams with John McLaughlin and Larry Young. Its loud organ-guitar-drums format became one of the early statements linking jazz improvisation with rock volume and energy.
label: information
